title: Spin-polarized lasing in a photonic lattice
authors: A. Herrero Otermin, N. Carlon Zambon, A. Bieganowska, F. Jabeen, L. Viña, C. Antón-Solanas
abstract: We characterize spin-polarized lasing in a two-dimensional photonic lattice fabricated from a GaAs/InGaAs semiconductor microcavity sample. The lattice is defined by a staggered arrangement of rounded rectangular micrometric mesas that laterally confine and couple the optical modes. Polarization-, angle-, and energy-resolved micro-photoluminescence measurements reveal the transition from the strong-coupling regime to photon lasing, accompanied by extended spatial coherence across several lattice unit cells. Under circularly polarized nonresonant excitation, the emitted light acquires a controllable circular polarization whose handedness follows that of the pump. These results establish photonic-lattice VCSELs as a platform for spin-controlled coherent emission in extended optical systems.
summary: This paper demonstrates how a 2D photonic lattice can be used to achieve spin-polarized lasing with extended spatial coherence. By controlling the pump polarization, the researchers can tune the handedness of the emitted light, providing a platform for spin-controlled coherent emission in large-scale optical systems.
main_problem: The study aims to develop a scalable, coherent light source with controllable polarization by engineering the photonic band structure of coupled microlaser arrays, addressing the loss of spatial coherence and multi-mode instabilities in large-area VCSELs.
main_result: The researchers demonstrated a transition from the strong-coupling (polariton) regime to the photon lasing regime in a 2D photonic lattice, achieving spin-controlled coherent emission where the light's circular polarization follows the pump's handedness.
method: The work combines electron-beam lithography and wet etching to fabricate a GaAs/InGaAs microcavity lattice, followed by micro-photoluminescence, polarization-resolved spectroscopy, and Michelson interferometry, complemented by a two-mode spin-laser rate equation model.
model_or_system: A 2D staggered square photonic lattice of rounded rectangular GaAs/InGaAs semiconductor microcavity mesas, which supports both exciton-polariton modes and photon lasing regimes.
key_observables: Energy- and momentum-resolved emission, full Stokes parameters (S0, S3), spatial coherence (g1), and Rabi splitting.
important_parameters: Lasing threshold (Pth ~ 0.3 mW/um2), Rabi splitting (3.35 meV), and TE-TM splitting (0.05 meV/um2).
main_assumptions: The theoretical model assumes a minimal two-mode system and neglects gain saturation effects, which could potentially hide bistability or chaos.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Schematic of the lattice design; Fig 2: Nonlinear input-output response and transition to the weak-coupling regime; Fig 3: Spatial coherence maps; Fig 4: Polarization maps (S0S3) and the dependence of S3 on pump power and QWP angle.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the challenge of large-area VCSEL stability, describes the fabrication and design of the 2D photonic lattice, presents experimental evidence of the polariton-to-lasing transition, analyzes the spin-control of polarization, and concludes with a theoretical model validating the observed spin-injection dynamics.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems as it demonstrates the manipulation of spin-polarized coherent states and the transition between different light-matter coupling regimes in an engineered lattice.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Sample design and simulated band structure of the staggered square lattice. (a) Schematic of the semiconductor microcavity used in this work. The structure consists of a 𝜆-thick GaAs cavity formed by two GaAs/AlAs distributed Bragg reflectors (20 and 24 DBR pairs) embedding a single In0.06Ga0.94As QW. The cavity spacer is patterned into a staggered square lattice of micrometer-scale mesas (∼2 × 3 𝜇m2) connected by narrow links that enable evanescent coupling between sites, forming a two-dimensional photonic lattice. Spin polarization of the gain medium via a circularly polarized nonresonant optical pump (𝜎±) enables lasing in an extended mode with a well-defined emission handedness....

Fig. 2. Nonlinear input-output response. (a–c) Energy- and momentum-resolved emission of the system as a function of increasing excitation power. (a) Low-density regime, showing the lower (LP) and upper (UP) polariton branches. The dashed white line in panel (a) indicates the exciton energy at 1.465 ± 0.001 eV. (b) Transition to the weak-coupling regime and onset of stimulated emission. (c) VCSEL regime. The arrows indicate the lasing from points in the dispersion relation with negative curvature. (d) Energy at the negative curvature points in the dispersion as a function of input power density; the error bars represent the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the spectrum at these points....

Fig. 3. Spatial coherence via Michelson interferometry in an extended lattice. Real space emission and 𝑔(1) measured in the polariton gas regime (a,b) and in the strong-coupling regime with a power of 0.29𝑃th and (c,d) VCSEL regime with a power of 1.15𝑃th. The intensity distribution in panels (a,c) is normalized to unity. The real space map of 𝑔(1) is extracted via off-axis holographic techniques.

Figure 3 shows the emission intensity and first-order spatial coherence 𝑔(1) maps at two excitation regimes: low density and above the lasing threshold, corresponding to pump powers of 0.29𝑃th and 1.15𝑃th for the panels (a,b) and (c,d), respectively. At low excitation power,

The 𝑆0 intensity map has been normalized to unity (𝑆0) prior to the representation of the 𝑆0𝑆3 map, hence re-scaling its false color scale. The black rectangles in both panels indicate the region of interest where 𝑆3 is averaged for the data points represented in the next panel. (c) Average 𝑆3 as a function of the QWP angle of the excitation and the laser power density for three values, 0.13𝑃th, 0.43𝑃th and 0.98𝑃th.
